narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path despite challenges, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admission system uses a model trained on data from many students' academic records, family backgrounds, and test scores to predict their chances of passing the bar exam on the first try—because law schools aim to admit those most likely to succeed. For you, the model estimated an 82.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger a rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factors pushing toward this "fail" prediction were your Year 3 school grade decile, which placed you in the bottom group compared to most students' mid-range positions, strongly signaling lower academic readiness. Your Year 1 grade decile, also at the bottom compared to typical students, reinforced this concern as the second-most influential factor. Your LSAT score of 28, well below the average of around 37, added further weight toward the failure prediction, as higher scores are strongly linked to bar success in the data. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.10, though below the 3.21 average, slightly nudged the prediction toward passing, as did your plan to attend full-time—like most students—and your lower-middle family income bracket. Overall, these academic indicators, especially your lower deciles and LSAT, outweighed the positives and set you apart from typical admitted students who rank higher in school grades and tests. This isn't a judgment on your potential or the barriers you may have faced as a Black woman; it's just how this model weighed the patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent grades to improve your school deciles, or exploring programs that support students from similar backgrounds with bar prep resources. You have the drive—many have overcome similar hurdles—and targeting these areas could open doors next time. Keep pushing; your story is still being written.
